inputSnapshot.summary: Operational dashboard for monitoring AI agents, deployments, CI/CD pipeline health, and GitHub repository status. Provides a real-time overview for teams running multiple AI agents across multiple repositories.
targetUsers: AI developers managing multiple agent deployments
coreFeatures: GitHub repository health overview (CI status, open PRs, failing checks)
Agent deployment status tracker (Ice, Lava, Stone agents)
Pipeline run history with pass/fail trends
Alert system for CI failures and deployment issues
Real-time refresh with configurable polling interval
constraints: GitHub API rate limits (5000 req/hour authenticated)
Must work with GitHub token (no OAuth required)
nonFunctionalRequirements: Sub-second UI response for cached data
Graceful degradation when GitHub API is slow

phaseRationale: The current input looks exploratory and low-risk.

architectureOptions.name: Lean Modular Monolith
architectureOptions.summary: One deployable application with explicit domain modules and a single primary data store.
strengths: Fastest path to a coherent first release.
tradeoffs: Harder to isolate workloads if scale diverges later.
Governance boundaries rely more on discipline than on topology.

architectureOptions.name: Modular Monolith With Background Jobs
architectureOptions.summary: Single primary deployable unit with explicit modules plus a worker path for async workflows and integrations.
strengths: Balances fast delivery with explicit async workflow support.
Keeps the system operable without early service sprawl.
Supports clearer control points for integrations and audit workflows.
tradeoffs: Slightly more moving parts than a pure monolith.
Still requires later extraction if independent scaling becomes dominant.
Async worker infrastructure may be premature if workflows stay simple.

architectureOptions.name: Early Service Separation
architectureOptions.summary: Separate user-facing application, workflow engine, and integration boundary early for stronger isolation.
strengths: Stronger hard boundaries for scaling and ownership.
Can align better with strict isolation or governance requirements.
tradeoffs: Higher delivery and operational cost from the start.
Adds distributed failure modes before the product is proven.

architectureRecommendation.summary: Start with modular monolith as the default architecture.
reasons: This option offers the best balance between delivery speed and long-term maintainability.
It avoids premature distributed complexity while keeping room for future extraction.

adrCandidates.title: Initial Architecture Shape
adrCandidates.decision: Start with modular monolith as the default architecture.

adrCandidates.title: Primary Data Store
adrCandidates.decision: Use a relational primary data store unless the domain clearly requires a different model.

adrCandidates.title: Integration Strategy
adrCandidates.decision: Encapsulate third-party integrations behind internal modules and keep failure handling explicit.

tasks.title: Write project charter and architecture baseline
tasks.summary: Capture the product scope, users, constraints, architecture shape, and open questions.
tasks.problem: The project starts from rough requirements and needs a shared baseline before implementation can be reviewed or sequenced safely.
tasks.solution: Create the charter, architecture overview, and first ADRs so later execution work inherits explicit assumptions instead of guesswork.
acceptanceCriteria: The charter captures summary, users, features, constraints, and unresolved questions.
The architecture overview names a recommended starting shape and its tradeoffs.
Initial ADRs exist for the highest-leverage early decisions.
implementationNotes: Use the recommended architecture as the default, not as a final truth claim.
Keep open questions visible so downstream agents know what may still change.
Reference the applicable playbooks directly in the generated charter.

tasks.title: Set up repository and delivery baseline
tasks.summary: Create the repository structure, quality checks, and basic documentation needed for implementation.
tasks.problem: Execution work will fragment quickly if the repository, quality gates, and documentation expectations are not defined up front.
tasks.solution: Establish the test path, delivery workflow expectations, and starter documentation before feature branches accumulate drift.
acceptanceCriteria: A repeatable local test command exists for the project baseline.
Core delivery expectations are documented for humans and agents.
The repository has enough structure that the first implementation wave can begin without setup churn.
implementationNotes: Keep the baseline minimal but reviewable.
Prefer a small number of reliable checks over aspirational tooling that nobody runs.
Align branch and review behavior with the development workflow playbook.

tasks.title: Implement GitHub repository health overview (CI status, open PRs, failing checks)
tasks.summary: Design and implement the capability for: GitHub repository health overview (CI status, open PRs, failing checks).
tasks.problem: The product cannot satisfy its initial scope until GitHub repository health overview (CI status, open PRs, failing checks) exists as a reviewable, testable capability.
tasks.solution: Add a focused module for GitHub repository health overview (CI status, open PRs, failing checks) that matches the recommended modular monolith and keeps integration boundaries explicit.
files: lib/ai/types.ts — ChatMessage, DashboardContext interfaces + Zod schemas
acceptanceCriteria: The GitHub repository health overview (CI status, open PRs, failing checks) capability is available through the intended application surface.
Core validation, error handling, and persistence for GitHub repository health overview (CI status, open PRs, failing checks) are covered by tests.
implementationNotes: Start from domain rules and access constraints before UI or transport details.
Keep module boundaries explicit so later extraction remains possible if the system grows.
Update docs and tests in the same change instead of leaving them for cleanup.

tasks.title: Add integration and error-handling coverage
tasks.summary: Verify the critical path, failure handling, and integration boundaries with tests.
tasks.problem: The initial implementation backlog leaves room for silent regressions unless critical-path and error-path coverage are added deliberately.
tasks.solution: Add end-to-end and integration-focused verification around the user path, external boundaries, and failure handling assumptions.
acceptanceCriteria: Critical path behavior is exercised through automated tests.
Integration and error paths fail loudly instead of degrading silently.
Known edge cases from the first release plan are captured in test coverage.
implementationNotes: Bias toward tests that exercise contracts and failure semantics, not only happy-path rendering.
Keep fixtures readable so future backlog work can extend them safely.

executionWaves.goal: Lock scope, assumptions, and engineering baseline.
Deliver the first critical capabilities and required controls.
Expand feature coverage once the core path is in place.
Harden, verify, and prepare the system for release.

promptExports.title: Architecture Analysis
promptExports.purpose: Refine or challenge the recommended architecture using the generated options and risks.

promptExports.title: Execution Next Wave
promptExports.purpose: Guide an implementation agent through the next delivery wave and its dependencies.

handoffManifest.summary: Coordinate downstream planning, governance, and execution agents from a single generated manifest.
handoffManifest.policySummary: Use product profile policies for concurrency, blocker escalation, and approval gating.
handoffManifest.coordinationStrategy: Use planning review steps in parallel where possible, then move into execution on the first delivery wave.

steps.name: Review Architecture Direction
steps.objective: Challenge the default architecture and confirm whether the recommended option still fits the clarified scope.

successCriteria: The recommended option is either confirmed or replaced with an explicit rationale.
Key tradeoffs and module boundaries are clear enough to guide the first implementation wave.

approvalGate.reason: No explicit approval gate required beyond normal review.

steps.name: Execute First Delivery Wave
steps.objective: Implement the initial foundation tasks with the current architecture and control assumptions.

successCriteria: All tasks in wave-1 are either completed or explicitly re-scoped with reasons.
Dependency-sensitive work lands in a reviewable sequence without skipping tests or documentation.

approvalGate.reason: No explicit approval gate required beyond normal review.

risks: Third-party integrations may slow delivery or require more explicit failure handling than expected.
